name: PulseMap
tagline: News that shows what affects you now.
description: PulseMap turns breaking news into a personal impact map, showing users which stories actually matter to their lives based on location, commute, calendar, job, and interests. Instead of forcing people to sift through endless headlines, it ranks news by practical relevance, such as whether a transit strike will delay their route, a weather alert threatens their neighborhood, or a policy change affects their paycheck or school district. The app combines trusted reporting with contextual layers like live maps, timeline forecasts, and “what this means for you” summaries written in plain language. A commuter might open it at 7:30 a.m. and instantly see that a protest is blocking their usual train line, while a parent could learn that a school board vote changed lunch funding at their child’s school. What makes PulseMap unique is that it does not just summarize news; it translates news into daily consequences and next actions, helping users make faster decisions without doomscrolling. It also learns what types of alerts people ignore or rely on, so the feed becomes sharper over time and less noisy than traditional news apps.
features: The app builds a personal relevance score for each story using location, schedule, and topic preferences so users see the most useful news first.
It overlays breaking stories on an interactive map so users can immediately understand where events are happening and whether they intersect with their daily routines.
It generates plain-language impact briefs that explain what changed, who is affected, and what the user may need to do next.
It sends time-sensitive alerts tied to practical triggers, such as when a storm shifts toward a user’s neighborhood or a road closure threatens their commute.
It includes a follow-up tracker that updates ongoing stories in short milestones, so users do not have to reread long articles to stay informed.
audience: Urban and suburban adults ages 22-50 who juggle commuting, caregiving, and busy work schedules, especially people who want news that is useful rather than overwhelming. It is especially valuable for parents, shift workers, freelancers, and professionals who need fast situational awareness throughout the day.
useCases: Sarah just finished a stressful meeting and wants to know whether the subway delay she heard about will make her late for daycare pickup, so she opens PulseMap to see the disruption plotted against her commute route and receives a two-minute alternate plan.

Marcus is a freelance designer working from a cafe when severe weather starts moving across town, so he checks PulseMap to see when the storm will reach his neighborhood, whether his power grid is at risk, and if he should head home early.

Aisha gets a notification that a city council vote could change parking rules near her apartment, so she opens PulseMap to read a plain-language summary, view the affected blocks on a map, and decide whether to submit a comment before the deadline.
